coredata.dc:title: Double regulation of bismuth and halogen source for the preparation of bismuth oxybromide nanosquares with enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yiling
Di, Jun
Ji, Mengxia
Gu, Kaizhi
Yin, Sheng
Li, Weibin
Xia, Jiexiang
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description:
In this paper, bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) nanosquares photocatalysts were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method with the double regulation of the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide and ammonium bismuth citrate (BCA). To the best of our knowledge, this report is the first to describe the BiOBr material with simultaneous bismuth and halogen bidirectional source regulation. The structures, components, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared samples were specifically explored. The photocatalytic ability was assessed using the degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. The BiOBr-IL+BCA exhibited improved photocatalytic activity compared with the BiOBr materials without double regulation. The primary active species were determined to be holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (
O
2
−
) using electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis and free radical trapping experiments. This enhanced activity was attributed to its larger specific surface, the superior electron transfer ability, and the increased negative conduction band position, which favors the photogenerated electrons to trap the molecular oxygen to produce
O
2
−
. The production of more
O
2
−
can benefit the removal of pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Double regulation
BiOBr
Nanosquares
Photocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: XPS valence spectra analysis and probable mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared BiOBr samples.
XPS patterns of the BiOBr-IL+BCA and BiOBr-KBr samples. (a) Survey of the sample; (b) Bi 4f; (c) O 1s; (d) Br 3d.
SEM and TEM images of the BiOBr-IL+BCA.
Raman spectra of the as-prepared BiOBr samples.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) (αEphoton)1/2 vs Ephoton curves of the as-prepared samples.
Nitrogen absorption-desorption isotherm of BiOBr samples.
Transient photocurrent of BiOBr-KBr and BiOBr-IL+BCA samples.
Steady-state PL spectra of BiOBr-KBr and BiOBr-IL+BCA samples.
Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of different BiOBr samples under visible light irradiation.
ESR spectra of BiOBr-KBr and BiOBr-IL+BCA samples.
Trapping experiment of BiOBr-IL+BCA during the degradation process.
XPS valence spectra of BiOBr-KBr and BiOBr-IL+BCA samples.

head.ce:title: Double regulation of bismuth and halogen source for the preparation of bismuth oxybromide nanosquares with enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth and halogen source bidirectional regulation has been employed to prepare the BiOBr nanosquares, which displayed improved photocatalytic activity.

In this paper, bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) nanosquares photocatalysts were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method with the double regulation of the ionic liquid (IL) 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide and ammonium bismuth citrate (BCA). To the best of our knowledge, this report is the first to describe the BiOBr material with simultaneous bismuth and halogen bidirectional source regulation. The structures, components, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic properties of the as-prepared samples were specifically explored. The photocatalytic ability was assessed using the degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. The BiOBr-IL+BCA exhibited improved photocatalytic activity compared with the BiOBr materials without double regulation. The primary active species were determined to be holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (
O
2
−
) using electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis and free radical trapping experiments. This enhanced activity was attributed to its larger specific surface, the superior electron transfer ability, and the increased negative conduction band position, which favors the photogenerated electrons to trap the molecular oxygen to produce
O
2
−
. The production of more
O
2
−
can benefit the removal of pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the unique properties of semiconductor photocatalysts have led to semiconductor photocatalysis for pollutant degradation, hydrogen production from water, and CO2 reduction into hydrocarbon fuels becoming increasingly popular [1–4]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been considered as one of the most promising photocatalysts for the degradation of different pollutants. However, the high recombination rate of electron-holes pairs and the wide band gap (3.2eV) result in the low quantum yield of light and poor efficiency in utilizing solar light, limiting its applications [5]. To overcome these drawbacks, several researchers have devoted to developing novel visible-light-response semiconductor materials, such as g-C3N4, Bi2WO6, and Bi2MoO6 [6–10].

Bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X=Cl, Br, and I) has the property of self-built, internal static electric fields due to the special layered structure, which features [Bi2O2] slabs interlaced with double halogen slabs, and has attracted more attention in the photocatalysis field [11–13]. Among the BiOX, bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) has attracted significant attention owing to the proper energy band positions and the excellent photocatalytic properties under visible light irradiation. However, the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs limits the activity of BiOBr [14–17]. To improve the performance of BiOX in the photocatalysis field, several tactics have been proposed, such as coupling with other semiconductor materials to prepare hybrid materials, regulating the morphologies of the photocatalysts or controlling the surface structure with different facet exposure, which endow them with completely different properties [18–25]. However, the photocatalytic activities of these materials are still poor and there is great urgency to find appropriate strategies to further boost the photocatalytic behavior of BiOBr.

Ionic liquids (ILs) have been receiving increased attention due to its unique properties. The characteristics including being odorless, nonflammable, good solubility, and good thermal and chemical stability allow ILs to be used in large scale production and will not create environmental pollution. During the synthesis process of the photocatalysts, ILs may simultaneously act as reactants, templates and solvents [26–28]. In this study, ILs were used instead of inorganic salts to synthesize the different sample, the thickness and shape of the materials were changed and the photocatalytic activity has been improved.

In previous studies, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O is typically used as the Bi source in the preparation of bismuth compounds. However, employing Bi(NO3)3·5H2O may frequently generate the agglomeration during the synthesis process. Bismuth citrate ammonium (BCA) has attracted significant interests due to its unique special structure. The [(NH3)2C6H7O7]3− organic anion of the BCA can affect the growth of the crystal structure of the samples and can sustain the transparency of the reaction solution in the synthesis process [29–32]. In this study, compared with the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, BCA as the bismuth source can be used to synthesize the samples with a larger specific surface area and square-like nanostructure, which is important for the photocatalysis process.

In this study, BiOBr samples were prepared via a facile hydrothermal method with the ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([C16mim]Br) and BCA bidirectional regulation. The [C16mim]Br acted not only as the Br source but also as the template at the system. The replacement of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O by the BCA can affect the shape of the samples. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was assessed with the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The results indicate that the combination of [C16mim]Br and BCA can regulate the sample morphology, increase the specific surface area, improve the separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and ultimately result in an enhanced photocatalytic activity.
All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received without purification. The ionic liquid [C16mim]Br (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd.

In a typical procedure, 1mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 2mmol KBr were dissolved into 20ml distilled water and the pH of the suspension was adjusted to 1 using nitric acid while continuously stirring. After the mixture stirred for 30min, the precursor was transferred into a 25ml Teflon-lined autoclave and subsequently heated at 140°C for 24h. The precipitate was separated by centrifuge and washed with distilled water and ethanol several times. Then, the product was dried at 50°C for 12h and was denoted as BiOBr-KBr. In keeping with the above process, using [C16mim]Br instead of KBr, the product was denoted as BiOBr-IL. Using 1mmol of BCA instead of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O produced the product denoted as BiOBr-BCA. BCA and [C16mim]Br were used instead of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and KBr, respectively, and the product was denoted as BiOBr-IL+BCA.

The structure and crystalline phase of the as prepared samples were determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) using a Shimadzu XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source at 20kV was used to learn the chemical states and elements of the as prepared samples. The morphology of the samples was determined through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JEOL JSM-7001F) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscope and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2010). The absorption properties were explored via a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer) using the diffuse reflectance method with BaSO4 powder as the substrate. After that, the band gaps of the materials were calculated through the Tauc equation. The surface areas of the samples were analyzed via the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms collected on a TriStar II 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as prepared samples were detected using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were checked on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer.

The photocurrent experiment was performed to explore the transfer efficiency of the photogenerated charge carries, which were produced by the as prepared samples under visible light irradiation. The photocurrent experiment systems were equipped with an electrochemical working station (CHI660B, Chen Hua Instruments), a 500W Xenon lamp and a standard three-electrode configuration. In the three-electrode system, a platinum wire was used as counter electrode, the saturated Ag/AgCl electrode was used as reference electrode and the indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass with the as-prepared samples were used as the working electrode. In the photocurrent experiment, 2mg of each as-prepared sample was dispersed in 200μL of ethylene glycol and 200μL of ethanol, and then dissolved by ultrasonication. Then, 20μL of the suspension was spread on the ITO glass electrode with a fixed area of 0.5×1cm2. The photocurrent was tested in a phosphate buffer with a concentration of 0.1M and a pH of 7 under irradiation from the Xenon lamp.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was assessed using the degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The light source was provided by a 300W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter to guarantee visible light irradiation. During the photocatalytic activity test, 20mg of the as-prepared photocatalyst was added into a Pyrex photocatalytic reactor contained with 100mL, 10mgL−1 RhB. While the solutions were being stirred, an air pump was used to supply the oxygen and a circulating water system was employed to avoid thermal catalytic effects. The mixture was stirred for 30min in darkness to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Next, a 3mL suspension was sampled and centrifuged to obtain a clear liquid at certain time intervals under visible light irradiation. The absorbance was measured with the characteristic band at 553nm with the UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu).

The structure and crystalline phase of the as-prepared samples were analyzed with X-ray power diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks of the XRD patterns from the samples correspond to the standard card of BiOBr (JCPDS No. 73-2061). According to Fig. 1, the main structure of the crystal has not changed, while the intensity has undergone some changes of the samples with the different precursors. The introduction of the BCA results in the crystallinity of the as-prepared samples to increase. However, using [C16mim]Br instead of KBr could cause a decrease of the crystallinity. Furthermore, no other crystal phases were found to exist, indicating that the samples synthesized were pure phase.

XPS was performed to investigate the surface composition and chemical states of the samples. As is shown in Fig. 2 , the survey scan of the BiOBr-IL+BCA and BiOBr-KBr samples reveals that Bi, O and Br are the primary elements of the as-prepared samples. The high resolution spectra in Fig. 2b show the binding energy of the Bi 4f was located at 159.6 and 164.7eV, indicating that Bi exists as Bi3+ in the BiOBr. The binding energy at 530.3 and 68.6eV correspond to the O 1s and Br 3d, respectively (Fig. 2c and d). The peaks of Bi 4f, O 1s and Br 3d in the as-prepared BiOBr-IL+BCA sample show a weak shift compared to the BiOBr-KBr sample. This indicates that the [C16mim]Br and BCA used as reactants can introduce variation in the chemical environment of the as-prepared BiOBr samples.

The morphology of the samples is studied through scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As is shown in Fig. S1 and S2, the BiOBr-KBr is composed of several nanosheets and disk-like features with the thickness approximately 80nm are present in the structure. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, after the introduction of the [C16mim]Br and BCA, the disk-like shapes gradually became nanosquares with a decreased thickness. The EDS analysis of the BiOBr-IL+BCA reveal that the Bi, O and Br are the primary elements of the as-prepared BiOBr-IL+BCA material (Fig. S3).

To analyze the role of the Bi and Br sources regulation, a TEM analysis of the samples was performed. The introduction of [C16mim]Br leads to a decrease in the sample thickness and has limited changes to the disk-like features, as illustrated in Fig. S4. Using the BCA instead of the Bi(NO3)3·5H2O to synthesize the sample, the shape of the nanosheets were transformed into square-like features, as seen in Fig. S5. Based on the above effects, after the simultaneous introduction of [C16mim]Br and BCA, both the thickness and shape of the sample was changed. The BiOBr-IL+BCA contains numerous nanosquares with at thickness of approximately 25nm as depicted in Fig. 3c and d. In general, squares have a larger surface area compared to circles of the same volume. Meanwhile, the decreased thickness of the BiOBr-IL+BCA could further enable a larger surface area. Generally, the larger surface area can supply more active sites in the photocatalysis and affect the properties of the materials. Additionally, the thinner thickness of the photocatalyst provides a shorter distance for the rapid transfer of the photogenerated electron from the bulk to the surface active sites and benefits the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [33,34].

The Raman spectra were investigated to further explore the surface microstructure of the as-prepared BiOBr samples. As shown in Fig. 4 , three peaks located at 57, 112, and 160cm−1 can be seen, which correspond to the external A1g, internal A1g, and internal E1g Bi-Br stretching modes, respectively. The intensity of the peaks decreased when changing the precursors to IL as the halogen or BCA as the bismuth source. The BiOBr-IL+BCA exhibits the weakest intensity, which demonstrates that the sample has both a smaller size and thickness and are in agreement with the TEM analysis.

To explore the optical absorption ability of the different BiOBr samples, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was conducted. The absorption edges of the samples introduce the [C16mim]Br or BCA were slightly blueshifted compared to the BiOBr-KBr in the visible light region, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The absorption edge of the BiOBr-IL+BCA is approximately 430nm, which was the narrowest one among the as-prepared BiOBr materials. The band gaps (shown in Fig. 5b) of the as-prepared samples were calculated from the Tauc equation and were approximately 2.74, 2.76, 2.78, and 2.79eV for BiOBr-KBr, BiOBr-IL, BiOBr-BCA, and BiOBr-IL+BCA, respectively. Usually, a narrow band gap is beneficial for the photocatalytic activity in the visible light region. The fact that the band gaps for the different materials were similar illustrates that the absorption ability may be not the main factor that effects the photocatalytic properties.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms test was employed to analyze the surface areas of the as-prepared samples (Fig. 6 ). The specific surface area of the BiOBr-KBr, BiOBr-IL, BiOBr-BCA and BiOBr-IL+BCA were calculated to be 0.39, 2.55, 3.32, 5.87 m2 g−1, respectively. A larger specific surface area of the samples can provide more active sites and improve the photocatalytic activity. In this study, the introduction of the [C16mim]Br or BCA led to an increase in the specific surface area of the samples. The BiOBr-IL+BCA achieved the largest specific surface area using the [C16mim]Br as the Br source and BCA as the Bi source, simultaneously. The improved surface area may be one of the significant factors that affect the photocatalytic activity.

The transient photocurrents experiment was performed to explore the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the photocurrent intensity of the BiOBr-IL+BCA was highest among all the samples and almost twice as high as the BiOBr-KBr. The higher photocurrent represents a higher separation efficiency of the electrons and holes and thus results in the improved photocatalytic activity [37]. Therefore, the BiOBr-IL+BCA should possess superior photocatalytic properties over the BiOBr-KBr.

To further investigate the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the photocatalytic process, the photoluminescence (PL) experiment was conducted. In the PL spectra, the higher relative intensity indicates the higher recombination of electrons and holes that are produced by photocatalysis under light irradiation [38–40]. As is shown in Fig. 8 , the BiOBr-IL+BCA has a lower relative intensity compared to BiOBr-KBr. This result demonstrates that the BiOBr-IL+BCA achieves a higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs and is in agreement with the results of the transient photocurrent.

Rhodamine B (RhB) was selected as the target pollutant to determine the photocatalytic ability of the different as-prepared samples under visible light irradiation. The suspension was stirred for 30min in darkness to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium before supplying the visible light irradiation. Then, extractions were made every 15min from the photocatalytic reactor. The photocatalytic properties of the different samples are depicted in Fig. 9 . After visible light irradiation for 60min, the removal efficiency of the RhB reached 97% by the BiOBr-IL+BCA. Within the same time, 43.7%, 55.7%, 84.1% of RhB can be degraded by BiOBr-KBr, BiOBr-IL and BiOBr-BCA samples, respectively. At the same time, the BiOBr-IL+BCA materials also displayed higher activity when compare to our previous reported BiOBr microspheres and g-C3N4 materials. The above results reveal that the BiOBr-IL+BCA materials are the outstanding photocatalysts and may be further used for practical pollutant removal.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment was performed to investigate the active species produced during the photocatalysis process under visible light irradiation. The characteristic peaks of the superoxide free radicals (O2
−) and hydroxyl free radicals (OH) can be detected by adding 5,5′-dimethyl-1-pirroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a scavenger with methanol and aqueous dispersions, respectively. As described in Fig. 10 a, a negligible O2
− signal is produced for the BiOBr-KBr. However, a significant O2
− signal is observed in the presence of the BiOBr-IL+BCA, as shown in Fig. 10c. However, both with or without the light irradiation, almost none of the OH signal is detected both for the BiOBr-IL+BCA and BiOBr-KBr, as shown in Fig. 10b and d. The outcome indicates that employing BCA and IL as the Bi and Br sources, respectively, could induce the formation more O2
−, which may favor the subsequent photodegradation process.

Trapping experiments were conducted to determine the reactive active species generated during the photocatalysis process by adding different radical scavengers. In this test, benzoquinone (BQ), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na) and tert-butanol were used as radical scavengers for the O2
−, h+ and OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11 , the degradation efficiency of the RhB by BiOBr-IL+BCA is inhibited significantly by adding the EDTA-2Na and BQ. The tert-butanol has a limited influence on the photocatalytic activity. The results suggest that h+ and O2
− radicals are the main reactive active species during the degradation process, which is in agreement with the ESR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS valence spectra analysis and probable mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the intrinsic reasons of the enhanced O2
− content, the XPS valence spectra was conducted. The valance band potential (EVB ) of the BiOBr-IL+BCA was measured to be 1.76eV and the BiOBr-KBr was 2.12eV (Fig. 12 ). Furthermore, the conduction band potential (ECB ) of the as-prepared materials can be calculated via the formula ECB
= EVB
− Eg . Similarly, The ECB of the BiOBr-IL+BCA and BiOBr-KBr are determined to be approximately −1.03 and −0.62eV, respectively. The higher CB position of the BiOBr-IL+BCA can enable the improved activation of O2 to O2
−.

Based on the above results, there is a probable illustration of the reaction mechanism concerning the ascendant photocatalytic activity of the BiOBr-IL+BCA under visible light irradiation. First, the morphology of the BiOBr-IL+BCA was altered by changing the precursor to both IL and BCA. The introduction of the BCA and IL led to the morphological change from disk-like to square-like and the thickness of the BiOBr-IL+BCA was reduced. This unique structure enabled the BiOBr-IL+BCA to have a larger surface area, which could adsorb more active species and better contact with the pollutant. At the same time, the thinner BiOBr-IL+BCA can more rapidly transfer the photogenerated electrons from the bulk to surface reactive sites and can promote the separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The higher separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs is significant for the increased photocatalytic activity. Second, the EVB and ECB positions of the samples are very important for the photocatalytic activity. Benefiting from the unique structure, the ECB of the BiOBr-IL+BCA becomes more negative than the E0 (O2/O2
−)=−0.046eV, which is sufficient to activate the oxygen and reduce it into O2
−. After that, the O2
− reacts with the pollutants and purifies the water. The higher CB position of the BiOBr-IL+BCA results in the production of more O2
− and thus may boost the photocatalytic activity.

BiOBr nanosquare materials were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method with IL and BCA double regulation for the first time. The morphology, structures and the band positions of the samples were adjusted via changing the precursors. The IL and BCA double regulation could change the disk-like features to square-like with smaller thicknesses. Among the as-prepared samples, the BiOBr-IL+BCA exhibited the best photocatalytic property for the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The enhanced activity of the BiOBr-IL+BCA may be attributed to the synergistic effect of the unique structure, enhanced BET surface area and higher charge separation efficiency. It is hoped that this double regulation strategy can be extended to the purpose of preparing other photocatalysts with high activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123) and the Doctoral Innovation Fund of Jiangsu Province (KYZZ16_0340).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.12.026.
